title: Red Wine on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Red wine contains anthocyanins (red-purple pigments), condensed tannins, and tartaric acid. Anthocyanins bond to upholstery immediately through hydrogen bonding and color intensifies with pH changes — alkaline conditions turn wine stains blue-purple and more difficult to remove. The tannin component oxidizes and darkens over time, cross-linking with the fiber polymer.
whyThisWorks: The acidic tannin spotter maintains correct pH (acidic) which keeps anthocyanins in their most removable red form on upholstery. The oxidizer then breaks the chromophore bonds in the anthocyanin molecule. Avoiding alkaline products is critical — ammonia, baking soda, and alkaline detergents permanently darken wine stains by shifting the anthocyanin into its blue form.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

homeSolutions.instruction: Upholstery is different from carpet: the wrong cleaner can stain worse than the spill. Find the manufacturer's tag (usually sewn into a seam under a cushion). Look for a single letter: W = water-safe, S = solvent only (dry cleaning), WS = both okay, X = vacuum only (professional treatment). If it's S or X, stop here and call a pro. If it's W or WS, continue. No tag? Treat as S (solvent only) — safer.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden section
homeSolutions.instruction: Flip the cushion over, or find the back of the frame, and dab a drop of the Dawn + 3% peroxide solution from step 5. Blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the fabric holds its color, you're safe. If dye transfers or the color changes, stop here — upholstery dye is usually far more delicate than carpet dye and home chemistry can lighten it. Skip to step 9.

homeSolutions.agent: The tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: Red wine is a tannin stain. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most "all-purpose" upholstery sprays — permanently lock and darken tannin on upholstery fiber. Use only: cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and white vinegar. Forget the salt trick — it doesn't help and can leave crystals in the fiber.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot with pressure — never rub, never saturate
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a stack of clean white paper towels or a white cloth straight down on the spill. Work from the outside of the stain toward the center. Keep going — use fresh sections each time — until the cloth stops picking up red. Upholstery is trickier than carpet because the cushion foam absorbs liquid FAST and hides it from you. More blotting, less rubbing, is always the right answer.

homeSolutions.agent: Damp-cloth chemistry — never pour on upholstery
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, WRING NEARLY DRY, and press onto the stain — work from outside edges in. This is the #1 difference from carpet: you never pour liquid on upholstery. Wet the cloth, not the cushion. Let it sit 5–10 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Most fresh wine lifts in 2–3 cycles.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach — dangerous gases. On dark/patterned upholstery, the patch test is your only safety net.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar into one cup of cold water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the area. Vinegar neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps remaining tannin in its lighter (red) form as it dries. Blot dry with a fresh clean cloth.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry with a fan — never a dryer or hair dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Blot until no more moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Then: point a fan at the treated area for 4–8 hours. Good airflow drying prevents mildew inside the cushion and stops water rings from forming at the edge of the damp zone. Don't use a hair dryer (heat sets tannin) and don't sit on the cushion until fully dry.

homeSolutions.agent: Daylight inspect — repeat once if needed
homeSolutions.instruction: Once fully dry, inspect in natural light. Wine often looks gone when damp and returns as a pink ring when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 a second time. Two rounds is the home ceiling on upholstery. If a brown halo has appeared (rather than pink), the tannin has polymerized — escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional upholstery cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If the care code is S or X, the patch test failed, or the stain survived two home rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Red wine on [W/WS/unknown] upholstery, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. No alkaline products used, no pouring — damp-cloth only." They have low-moisture hot-water extraction and commercial tannin formulas that don't over-saturate the foam.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If red wine stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is red wine on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
